questions.question: Describe the three phases of Merge Sort that follow the divide‑and‑conquer paradigm. Give the overall time‑complexity, auxiliary space requirement, and state whether the sort is stable.
keyPoints: Divide: split the array into two roughly equal halves.
Combine: merge the two sorted halves in linear time.
Time O(n log n), auxiliary space O(n), stable.

questions.question: Name three pivot‑selection strategies used in Quick Sort and discuss how each influences the worst‑case and average‑case running time.
keyPoints: First element as pivot – O(n^2) worst‑case on already sorted input.
Last element as pivot – same worst‑case as first element.
Median‑of‑three – reduces likelihood of worst‑case, expected O(n log n).
Random pivot – expected O(n log n) and avoids pathological cases.

questions.question: Compare Merge Sort and Quick Sort with respect to algorithm design, time and space complexity, stability, and typical use cases. Provide examples of real‑world applications where each is preferred.
keyPoints: Both use divide‑and‑conquer; Merge Sort merges, Quick Sort partitions.
Merge Sort: O(n log n) time, O(n) auxiliary space, stable; Quick Sort: average O(n log n), worst O(n^2), in‑place O(log n) stack, not stable.
Merge Sort preferred for linked lists, external sorting, and when stability is required.
Quick Sort preferred for in‑memory array sorting where average performance matters, e.g., C's qsort, Java primitive arrays.

questions.question: Derive the average‑case recurrence for Quick Sort and solve it to obtain O(n log n). Discuss the partition step and its effect on the recursion depth and auxiliary stack space.
keyPoints: Average case recurrence T(n) = (2/n) Σ_{k=0}^{n‑1} T(k) + Θ(n).
Solved via recursion‑tree or substitution yields T(n) = O(n log n).
Partition takes Θ(n) time and creates two sub‑problems of roughly equal size on average.
Average recursion depth O(log n), so in‑place Quick Sort uses O(log n) auxiliary stack space; worst‑case depth O(n).
